metadata.title: Accelerated Decline in Hepatic Glucose Production During Fasting in Normal Women Compared With Men
metadata.study_design: Comparative metabolic study with sequential 3H-glucose infusions after 14 and 64-hour fasts
metadata.demographics: Five normal men and five normal women in the midfollicular phase of their menstrual cycle, matched for age and body mass index, with no history of diabetes mellitus, hypertension, or kidney disease

evidence.quote: Accelerated Decline in Hepatic Glucose Production During Fasting in Normal Women Compared With Men
John N. Clore, Penny S. Glickman, Shirley T. Helm, John E. Nestler, and William G. Blackard
five normal men and five normal women underwent sequentiel S3H-glucose infusions after both a 14 and a 64hour fast

Five of the subjects were male and five were female in the midfollicular phase of their menstrual cycle. None had a history of diabetes mellitus, hypertension, or kidney disease or were on any medications. Subjects were matched for age and body mass index (BMI).
five normal men and five normal women underwent sequentiel S3H-glucose infusions

methods_participants.label: Inclusion criteria - health status
methods_participants.value: Normal volunteers with no history of diabetes mellitus, hypertension, or kidney disease and not on any medications

evidence.quote: None had a history of diabetes mellitus, hypertension, or kidney disease or were on any medications.
A primed (25 µCi) continuous (0.25 µCi) infusion of 3-3H-glucose (New England Nuclear, Boston) was administered via a forearm vein from 7 AM until 10 AM

methods_participants.label: Blood sampling timing - glucose kinetics measurement

evidence.quote: No blood samples were obtained during the two-hour equilibration period from 7 AM to 9 AM, during which time steady state was achieved.

methods_participants.label: Free fatty acid measurement method
methods_participants.method: direct radioimmunoassay (Gammacoat assay, Clinical Assays, Cambridge, MA)

evidence.quote: Plasma glucose was measured immediately after centrifugation by the glucose oxidase method (Beckman, Fullerton, CA).
Plasma insulin, C-peptide, growth hormone, and glucagon were determined using double-antibody radioimmunoassays.

methods_participants.label: Body composition assessment method 1
methods_participants.method: skinfold measurements and impedance plethysmography (BIA-101 Body Composition Analyzer, RJL Systems, Detroit)

evidence.quote: Body composition was assessed by skinfold measurements and impedance plethysmography (BIA-101 Body Composition Analyzer, RJL Systems, Detroit)

methods_participants.label: Female participants - menstrual cycle phase
methods_participants.value: Subjects at bed rest during three hours of each study period but permitted to leave GCRC for usual daily activities in interim

evidence.quote: Following a meal at 6 PM the subjects were fasted until completion of the study (total fast 64 hour).
Comparisons between the two groups (women v men) were performed using Student's two-tailed unpaired t test
Statistical significance was assumed when P < .05

narrative_results.statement: Plasma glucose values fell by 24% from the 16th to the 64th hour of fasting in both men and women

evidence.quote: Plasma glucose values fell by 24% from the 16th to the 64th hour of fasting in both men (5.23 + 0.03 v 3.96 + 0.14 mmol/L, P < .OOl) and women (4.84 + 0.14 v 3.65 f 0.25 mmol/L, P < .OOl).

narrative_results.statement: Plasma glycerol levels were significantly greater in women compared with men after the 64-hour fast

evidence.quote: Glucose clearance rates did not change significantly in either sex after the 64-hour fast

narrative_results.statement: Changes in plasma glucose were associated with decreases in plasma insulin and C-peptide as well as increases in plasma glucagon

evidence.quote: Changes in plasma glucose were associated with decreases in plasma insulin and C-peptide as well as increases in plasma glucagon (Table 2).

narrative_results.statement: 64-hour fasted women exhibit a greater fall in HGO than do men similarly fasted

evidence.quote: In conclusion, 64-hour fasted women exhibit a greater fall in HGO than do men similarly fasted.

discrepancies.pass_2_value: Pass 1 describes infusion duration from 7 AM to 10 AM (3 hours), while Pass 2 specifies equilibration period of 2 hours. These appear to describe different aspects or conflicting timeframes.
Pass 1 states no blood samples from 7-9 AM during equilibration, while Pass 2 states blood sampling at 20-minute intervals from 9-10 AM. These describe different sampling protocols that may conflict.
Pass 1 describes sampling during 9-10 AM (20-minute intervals), while Pass 2 describes baseline sampling at 7 AM after fasting. These are different time points and sampling protocols.
Pass 1 describes C-peptide assay using double-antibody radioimmunoassay, while Pass 2 describes cortisol measurement using direct radioimmunoassay. These are different analytes and different methods.

Pass 1 describes cortisol assay method (direct radioimmunoassay), while Pass 2 describes body composition assessment (skinfold and impedance plethysmography). These are completely different measurements.

Pass 1 describes body composition assessment (skinfold measurements) while Pass 2 describes compliance assessment (urinary ketone determinations). These are different measurement purposes and methods.

Pass_1 reports plasma alanine concentrations fell similarly in men and women. Pass_2 reports plasma glycerol levels rose significantly. These are different metabolites with opposite directional changes.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Rates of glucose appearance (Ra) fell significantly in both men and women after 64-hour fast
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Rates of glucose appearance (Ra) fell significantly in both men and women during the 64-hour fast

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: The percent fall in Ra was significantly greater in women compared with men
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: The fall in Ra was significantly greater in women compared with men

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Glucose clearance rates did not change significantly in either sex after the 64-hour fast
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Glucose clearance rates did not change significantly in either sex after the 64-hour fast (Fig 1).

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: plasma glycerol levels were significantly greater in women compared with men after the 64-hour fast (P < .05).
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: The expected rise in glycerol concentrations in men (0.05 + 0.01 v 0.11 + 0.02 mmol/L, P < .05) and women (0.07 + 0.01 v 0.16 + 0.01 mmol/L, P-c .Ol) was observed.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Plasma glycerol levels were significantly greater in women compared with men after the 64-hour fast
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Plasma glycerol levels rose significantly in both men and women after the 64-hour fast

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Plasma FFA also rose significantly in men (0.49 + 0.06 v 0.8 1 f 0.09 mmol/L, P < .Ol) and women (0.47 + 0.04 v 0.89 2 0.04 mmol/L, P -c .05), without significant intersex differences.
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Moreover, plasma glycerol levels were significantly greater in women compared with men after the 64-hour fast (P < .05).

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Plasma FFA rose significantly in both men and women without significant intersex differences
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Plasma glycerol levels were significantly greater in women compared with men after the 64-hour fast

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Plasma alanine concentrations fell similarly in the men and women (Table 3)
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Plasma FFA also rose significantly in men (0.49 + 0.06 v 0.8 1 f 0.09 mmol/L, P < .Ol) and women (0.47 + 0.04 v 0.89 2 0.04 mmol/L, P -c .05), without significant intersex differences.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Plasma alanine concentrations fell similarly in men and women
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Plasma FFA rose significantly in both men and women without significant intersex differences

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: In conclusion, 64-hour fasted women exhibit a greater fall in HGO than do men similarly fasted.
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Although plasma β-hyroxybutyrate levels varied considerably both before and after the fast (Table 4), all subjects exhibited at least a sixfold increase during the fast.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: 64-hour fasted women exhibit a greater fall in HGO than do men similarly fasted
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: All subjects exhibited at least a sixfold increase in plasma β-hydroxybutyrate levels during the fast

semantic_groups.rationale: Pass 1 includes 'not on any medications' while Pass 2 omits this detail. Both describe the same health status criteria, but Pass 1 is more complete.
Both describe female subjects in midfollicular phase of menstrual cycle. Minor wording differences ('subjects' vs 'participants', different label structure) do not affect the underlying fact.
Both specify 14 hours fasting duration. Only difference is terminology ('first study' vs 'first measurement'), which is negligible.
Both specify 64 hours fasting duration. Only difference is terminology ('second study' vs 'second measurement'), which is negligible.
Pass 1 specifies priming dose of 25 µCi while Pass 2 describes 'primed continuous infusion' without the dose value. Both refer to 3-3H-glucose but with different detail levels.
Pass 1 contains information about continuous dose (0.25 µCi) while Pass 2 is empty. This fact appears only in Pass 1.
Pass 1 describes infusion duration from 7 AM to 10 AM (3 hours), while Pass 2 specifies equilibration period of 2 hours. These appear to describe different aspects or conflicting timeframes.
Pass 1 states no blood samples from 7-9 AM during equilibration, while Pass 2 states blood sampling at 20-minute intervals from 9-10 AM. These describe different sampling protocols that may conflict.
Pass 1 describes sampling during 9-10 AM (20-minute intervals), while Pass 2 describes baseline sampling at 7 AM after fasting. These are different time points and sampling protocols.

Both describe glucose oxidase method for plasma glucose measurement. Pass 1 includes manufacturer details (Beckman, Fullerton, CA) while Pass 2 mentions Beckman analyzer, but the core method is identical.
Both describe double-antibody radioimmunoassay for insulin measurement. Pass 2 abbreviates to 'radioimmunoassay' but refers to the same method.
Pass 1 describes C-peptide assay using double-antibody radioimmunoassay, while Pass 2 describes cortisol measurement using direct radioimmunoassay. These are different analytes and different methods.
Pass 1 contains growth hormone assay information, but Pass 2 is empty. This is an orphan entry from Pass 1.
Pass 1 contains glucagon assay information, but Pass 2 is empty. This is an orphan entry from Pass 1.

Pass 1 describes cortisol assay method (direct radioimmunoassay), while Pass 2 describes body composition assessment (skinfold and impedance plethysmography). These are completely different measurements.
Both describe enzymatic methods for free fatty acid measurement. Pass 2 abbreviates 'enzymatic' but refers to the same method as Pass 1.

Pass 1 describes body composition assessment (skinfold measurements) while Pass 2 describes compliance assessment (urinary ketone determinations). These are different measurement purposes and methods.
Pass 1 contains information about impedance plethysmography body composition assessment; Pass 2 is empty.
Pass 1 contains fasting protocol timing information; Pass 2 is empty.
Pass 1 contains baseline blood sampling timing; Pass 2 is empty.
Pass 1 contains activity level during study information; Pass 2 is empty.
Pass 1 contains fasting compliance assessment method; Pass 2 is empty.
Both describe the same participant count (5 men). Pass 1 states '5 men and 5 women' while Pass 2 specifies '5 normal men'. The male count is consistent; female information is separate.
Pass 2 contains information about female participants (5 normal women); Pass 1 is empty.
Pass 1 is empty/null while Pass 2 contains a specific inclusion criterion about medication status. No comparison possible.
Both describe Ra falling during a 64-hour fast in both sexes. 'After' vs 'during' represents negligible temporal distinction in this context.
Both convey that Ra fall was significantly greater in women than men. Pass_1 specifies 'percent fall' while pass_2 says 'fall'; both describe the same sex difference finding.
Identical statements about glucose clearance rates showing no significant change in either sex after 64-hour fast.
Both describe the same associations between plasma glucose changes and hormonal changes. Pass_2 adds a label '; association' but conveys identical information.
Both report plasma glycerol significantly greater in women vs men after 64-hour fast. Pass_1 uses 'women > men' notation while pass_2 uses 'sex difference' label; same finding.
Identical statements about plasma FFA rising significantly in both sexes without intersex differences.

Pass_1 reports plasma alanine concentrations fell similarly in men and women. Pass_2 reports plasma glycerol levels rose significantly. These are different metabolites with opposite directional changes.
Both describe greater fall in HGO in fasted women vs men. Pass_1 uses 'women > men' notation while pass_2 uses 'sex difference' label; same finding.
Identical text across both extractions describing the same plasma glucose measurement finding
Pass 1 contains no extraction while Pass 2 contains a valid statement about β-hydroxybutyrate levels; orphan entry from Pass 2
